post_content: Caveat emptor is a Latin phrase that translates to "let the buyer beware." It is a legal principle that places the responsibility on the buyer to examine and assess the quality, condition, and value of a product or service before making a purchase. Under this principle, the buyer assumes the risk for any defects or issues with the item unless the seller explicitly guarantees its condition or provides assurances.

post_content: Filter is an Indian web series that blends comedy and social commentary to tackle relatable, modern-day issues faced by young adults. post_content: The Harvest is a 2013 horror-thriller film directed by John McNaughton that delves into a dark family dynamic centered around a disturbing secret. The story follows Maryann, a young girl who moves to a new town and befriends Andy, a sickly boy who lives nearby and is isolated due to a rare illness. Andy’s parents, Katherine (Samantha Morton) and Richard (Michael Shannon), are overly protective, especially Katherine, who keeps tight control over her son’s life and forbids him from seeing anyone outside the family.

post_content: The Box is a dark, suspense-filled series that follows the story of a troubled young woman, Sharon Pici (played by Anna Friel), who is a Kansas City detective with an obsessive personality. post_content: Habeas corpus is a fundamental legal principle originating from English common law, often referred to as the “Great Writ of Liberty.” Latin for "you shall have the body," it is a legal procedure that protects individuals from unlawful detention. By invoking habeas corpus, a person detained or imprisoned can petition the court to determine whether their imprisonment is lawful.

post_content: The show centers around Mark Scout (played by Adam Scott), an employee of Lumon Industries, a company where employees undergo a surgical procedure known as "severance." This procedure splits their memories into two parts: the “work self” (Innie) and the “home self” (Outie). The “Innie” knows nothing of the outside world, while the “Outie” is unaware of what happens at work.
